questions.rationale: Insulin initiation is indicated when: A1c >10–12% at diagnosis, symptomatic hyperglycemia (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss), or when oral agents are insufficient. This patient meets all three. Additionally, low-normal C-peptide + weight loss + pending autoantibodies raises the possibility of LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults) or slowly progressive T1DM in a 44-year-old — insulin is appropriate pending autoantibody results. Insulin rapidly reduces glucose toxicity, which can then clarify insulin secretory capacity. Metformin alone (option A) would provide insufficient A1c reduction for this severity and does not address the metabolic emergency. Holding medications (option D) is inappropriate — hyperglycemia at this level requires immediate treatment. Starting empagliflozin (option B) in severe hyperglycemia carries DKA risk if any degree of insulin deficiency exists.

questions.stemAnchor: 82-year-old frail T2DM + A1c 9.1% + frequent falls + prior hypoglycemic episode — relaxed A1c target elderly
questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with T2DM (on glipizide 10 mg daily + metformin 500 mg twice daily) has an A1c of 9.1%. She has had two hypoglycemic episodes in the past 3 months (both requiring juice). She lives alone and has fallen three times in the past 6 months, including once during a hypoglycemic episode. She has moderate cognitive impairment (MMSE 20/30). Which of the following best represents the most appropriate A1c target and medication adjustment for this patient?

options.A: Intensify therapy to achieve A1c <7% — tighter control prevents microvascular complications
options.B: Maintain current regimen — A1c 9.1% is acceptable in any frail elderly patient
options.C: Accept a relaxed A1c target of <8.5% for this frail patient; discontinue or reduce glipizide (highest hypoglycemia risk) — consider switching to DPP-4 inhibitor for safety
options.D: Add insulin glargine — basal insulin is safer than sulfonylureas in frail elderly patients

questions.rationale: In a frail elderly patient (82 years, falls, cognitive impairment, prior hypoglycemic episodes), tight glycemic control causes more harm than benefit. ACCORD trial: intensive control (<6%) increased mortality in high-risk T2DM. For frail older adults: ADA guidelines recommend a relaxed target of <8.0–8.5% to minimize hypoglycemia risk, which in this patient causes falls — a major source of morbidity and mortality. The priority action: discontinue or significantly reduce glipizide (highest sulfonylurea hypoglycemia risk) and switch to a DPP-4 inhibitor (low independent hypoglycemia risk). Adding basal insulin (option D) would increase — not reduce — hypoglycemia risk in a patient already falling from hypoglycemia. Current glipizide (A1c 9.1% despite therapy) is not effective enough to justify its fall risk in this patient.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + metformin + eGFR declining 38 — metformin safety threshold in CKD
questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with T2DM has been on metformin 1000 mg twice daily for 4 years. He is referred for CKD management. His eGFR has declined from 62 to 38 mL/min over the past 18 months. He has no current symptoms. His A1c is 7.2% on metformin alone. Which of the following correctly describes the appropriate metformin management given his eGFR?

options.A: Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily — eGFR 38 is above the threshold for any concern
options.B: Discontinue metformin immediately — eGFR <45 is an absolute contraindication to metformin
options.C: Reduce metformin dose (e.g., to 500 mg twice daily), use with caution, and monitor eGFR every 3 months; discontinue if eGFR falls below 30
options.D: Switch to metformin extended-release (XR) — XR formulation is safe in CKD at all eGFR levels

questions.rationale: FDA 2016 updated labeling for metformin in CKD: eGFR ≥45 = safe to continue; eGFR 30–44 = use with caution (reduce dose, monitor closely); eGFR <30 = contraindicated due to lactic acidosis risk. This patient's eGFR is 38 — in the caution zone (30–44): reduce dose and monitor every 3 months. Option A: eGFR 38 is NOT above the threshold — it requires dose reduction. Option B: eGFR <45 is a caution/reduction zone, NOT absolute contraindication (that's <30). Option D: metformin XR has the same renal clearance mechanism as regular metformin and the same eGFR thresholds — it is not safer in CKD based on formulation.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM on metformin + saxagliptin + HF hospitalization in past year — DPP-4i HF risk SAVOR-TIMI
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with T2DM (A1c 7.8%) and HFpEF was recently hospitalized for HF decompensation. His current diabetes regimen is metformin 1000 mg twice daily + saxagliptin 5 mg daily. His cardiologist flagged the diabetes regimen. Which of the following correctly identifies the concern and most appropriate change?

options.A: Saxagliptin has no cardiac concerns — continue unchanged
options.B: Saxagliptin is associated with increased risk of HF hospitalization in T2DM patients — switch to a safer DPP-4 inhibitor such as linagliptin, or to a GLP-1 RA/SGLT-2i
options.C: Metformin is the medication causing fluid retention and HF — switch to sitagliptin monotherapy
options.D: Discontinue all diabetes medications until HF is stable; hyperglycemia is less concerning than HF decompensation

questions.rationale: SAVOR-TIMI trial: saxagliptin (a DPP-4 inhibitor) was associated with a statistically significant 27% increase in HF hospitalization compared to placebo in T2DM patients with established CV disease. This finding was confirmed by alogliptin in the EXAMINE trial. Mechanism unclear — possibly related to elevation of stromal cell-derived factor 1α. Linagliptin and sitagliptin do not appear to share this same HF hospitalization signal. In a patient with HFpEF + recent HF hospitalization, saxagliptin should be switched. Alternatives with favorable HF data: SGLT-2 inhibitors (EMPEROR-Preserved: empagliflozin reduces HF hospitalization in HFpEF — the preferred option) or linagliptin (neutral for HF). This question sits at the SF-01 boundary — noting which DPP-4i has HF signal is within the SF-01 slugA scope (agent-level selection WITHIN DPP-4 class for T2DM intensification; SGLT-2i cardiorenal data belongs to slugB).

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + renal failure eGFR 22 + glyburide prescribed — glyburide active metabolite accumulation in CKD
questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with T2DM and CKD (eGFR 22 mL/min) is transferred to your practice. Her previous NP had her on metformin (now discontinued) and glyburide 5 mg twice daily. Her A1c is 8.4%. Which of the following correctly identifies the safety concern with glyburide in this patient?

options.A: Glyburide is safe in CKD at all eGFR levels since it is hepatically metabolized
options.B: Glyburide is the safest sulfonylurea in CKD because it has the shortest half-life
options.C: Glyburide has active metabolites that accumulate in CKD — this is associated with prolonged and severe hypoglycemia; switch to glipizide (shorter-acting, no active metabolites) or insulin
options.D: Glyburide should be replaced by pioglitazone since TZDs have no renal clearance concerns

questions.rationale: Glyburide is the most nephrotoxic sulfonylurea — it has active metabolites (M1 and M2) that are renally cleared and accumulate in CKD, leading to prolonged and severe hypoglycemia. This is a well-documented, clinically important distinction within the sulfonylurea class: Glyburide = AVOID in CKD (especially eGFR <30); Glimepiride = use with caution in eGFR 30–45, avoid <30; Glipizide = PREFERRED sulfonylurea in CKD — inactivated in liver, no active renal metabolites. This patient on glyburide with eGFR 22 is at high risk for prolonged hypoglycemia. Switch to glipizide (5 mg daily, starting low) or basal insulin (glargine or detemir — both can be used in CKD). Pioglitazone (option D): fluid retention worsens CKD-related edema and is not preferred.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + starting basal insulin + dose titration algorithm — 15-15 rule basal titration
questions.stem: A 61-year-old woman with T2DM (A1c 9.8%) is not controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID + sitagliptin 100 mg daily. You initiate insulin glargine 10 units at bedtime. You explain the titration protocol: she should increase the dose by 2 units every 3 days until her fasting blood glucose (FBG) target is met. Which of the following FBG target is most appropriate for guiding basal insulin titration in a non-frail adult with T2DM?

options.A: FBG target: 70–100 mg/dL (strict, same as normal fasting range)
options.B: FBG target: 80–130 mg/dL — the ADA recommended preprandial target for most non-pregnant adults with T2DM
options.C: FBG target: 120–160 mg/dL — a higher target is safer for all patients on insulin
options.D: FBG target: 140–180 mg/dL — the ICU glycemic target applies to all insulin-managed patients

questions.rationale: ADA 2024 preprandial (fasting) glucose target for most non-pregnant adults with T2DM: 80–130 mg/dL. For basal insulin titration: increase glargine by 2 units every 3 days until FBG consistently reads 80–130 mg/dL. A strict target of 70–100 (option A) risks hypoglycemia with titration. The 120–160 range (option C) is used in frail elderly or high-risk patients. ICU target (option D) of 140–180 applies only to critically ill hospitalized patients — not appropriate for ambulatory T2DM management. The titration algorithm: if FBG >130 for 3 consecutive days → increase glargine by 2 units; if FBG <80 → reduce by 2–4 units and report to provider.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM follow-up + UACR 285 mg/g new microalbuminuria + eGFR 52 + on metformin only — ACEi/ARB initiation for DN
questions.stem: A 59-year-old man with T2DM (A1c 7.4% on metformin) and hypertension (BP 136/86 on amlodipine 10 mg) has a new UACR of 285 mg/g (macroalbuminuria) on this visit. Previous UACR 6 months ago was 38 mg/g. eGFR is 52 mL/min. He does not take an ACEi or ARB. Which of the following is the most appropriate addition to his medication regimen?

options.A: No medication change — UACR of 285 mg/g requires two consecutive measurements to confirm before treating
options.B: Add lisinopril 10 mg daily for renoprotection — ACEi/ARB reduces proteinuria and slows CKD progression in T2DM even in normotensive patients
options.C: Add furosemide to reduce the albuminuria through diuresis
options.D: Increase amlodipine to reduce BP further — additional BP lowering alone will resolve the proteinuria

questions.rationale: ACEi or ARB is the first-line therapy for any degree of persistent albuminuria in T2DM — specifically UACR ≥30 mg/g (microalbuminuria threshold) — regardless of blood pressure status. This patient now has macroalbuminuria (UACR 285 mg/g), representing significant diabetic nephropathy progression. ACEi (lisinopril) or ARB (losartan) reduces intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria by blocking angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriolar constriction — this is renoprotective independent of BP. Option A (wait for second confirmation) is appropriate for microalbuminuria (UACR 30–300), but macroalbuminuria (>300) or a rapid rise from 38 to 285 in 6 months warrants immediate action. Furosemide (C) reduces volume but does not address diabetic nephropathy. Amlodipine (D) does not provide the specific renoprotective RAAS effect.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM monitoring schedule — annual exam components including foot exam monofilament retinal exam
questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with T2DM (A1c 7.1%, on metformin and sitagliptin) has been with your practice for 3 years. She is well-controlled and asymptomatic. Review of her chart shows no dilated eye exam in 2 years, no foot exam documented in 12 months, no urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) in 18 months, and no LDL documented in 14 months. Which of the following most completely identifies the overdue monitoring interventions?

options.A: Only the A1c needs to be checked more frequently — the other tests are optional in well-controlled T2DM
options.B: Annual UACR + eGFR, annual comprehensive foot exam, annual dilated fundus exam, and annual fasting lipid panel are all overdue per ADA standards of care
options.C: Dilated eye exam every 3 years is the guideline — the 2-year gap is within range
options.D: Foot exams are only required in patients with established neuropathy or ulcer history

questions.rationale: ADA Standards of Diabetes Care annual monitoring checklist for T2DM: (1) A1c every 3–6 months (or annually if stable at goal), (2) UACR annually (to screen for diabetic nephropathy), (3) eGFR annually, (4) Dilated fundus exam annually (or every 2 years if no retinopathy found on 2 consecutive exams — but the 2-year gap here means an exam is due), (5) Comprehensive foot exam annually (10g monofilament + pulses + inspection) for all patients — not just those with established neuropathy, (6) Fasting lipid panel annually. All four overdue components (UACR, foot exam, eye exam, lipids) need to be addressed at this visit. Option C incorrectly extends the eye exam interval. Option D incorrectly restricts foot exams.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + statin indication — all T2DM 40-75 with ASCVD moderate statin; established ASCVD high statin
questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with T2DM is seen for a follow-up visit. Fasting LDL is 112 mg/dL, HDL 38, TG 210. He had an MI 3 years ago and is on aspirin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. His 10-year ASCVD risk is 28%. He is not on a statin. Which of the following best describes the correct statin therapy for this patient?

options.A: No statin is needed — LDL 112 mg/dL is borderline and the patient is already on multiple cardiac medications
options.B: Moderate-intensity statin (rosuvastatin 10 mg) — appropriate for T2DM age 40–75 without established ASCVD
options.C: High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) — established ASCVD is a compelling indication for high-intensity statin regardless of LDL
options.D: Low-intensity statin (rosuvastatin 5 mg) — lower dose preferred in diabetic patients to reduce myopathy risk

questions.rationale: Per 2018 ACC/AHA Cholesterol guidelines: patients with established clinical ASCVD (prior MI, stroke, ACS, coronary revascularization, PAD) are in the HIGHEST priority benefit group and require HIGH-INTENSITY statin therapy (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg — reduces LDL ≥50%). An LDL of 112 mg/dL in a patient with established ASCVD is significantly above target (<70 mg/dL for ASCVD, <55 mg/dL for 'very high risk' ASCVD). T2DM age 40–75 WITHOUT established ASCVD = moderate-intensity statin (option B) — but this patient HAS established ASCVD (prior MI), which overrides the T2DM-alone recommendation. Option A is incorrect — every patient with established ASCVD requires high-intensity statin. Low-intensity statin (option D) is only appropriate for elderly patients who cannot tolerate higher doses.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + neuropathic pain stocking glove + monofilament loss — DPN diagnosis and treatment options
questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman with T2DM (A1c 8.1%, on metformin) presents with bilateral foot and lower leg burning pain that is worse at night and interferes with sleep. She describes it as 'pins and needles constantly.' 10g monofilament testing reveals absent sensation at 3 sites on each foot. Vibration sense is diminished bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment for her diabetic peripheral neuropathy pain?

options.A: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily — the most accessible analgesic for neuropathic pain
options.B: Duloxetine 60 mg daily or pregabalin 75 mg twice daily — both are FDA-approved first-line agents for diabetic peripheral neuropathy pain
options.C: Tramadol 50 mg every 6 hours — opioids are first-line for neuropathic pain in diabetic patients
options.D: Gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime — the most cost-effective neuropathic pain agent with no titration required

questions.rationale: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN) pain management — FDA-approved first-line agents: (1) Duloxetine (SNRI): 60 mg daily, also helps with comorbid depression, FDA-approved for DPN; (2) Pregabalin (alpha-2-delta ligand): 75–150 mg twice daily, FDA-approved for DPN; (3) Tapentadol extended-release: FDA-approved for DPN but opioid concerns limit use to refractory cases. Gabapentin (option D) is commonly used off-label but requires slow titration to effective doses (1200–3600 mg/day) and is NOT FDA-approved for DPN. Ibuprofen (option A) does not work for neuropathic pain and is contraindicated in DM/CKD. Tramadol (option C) — opioids are last-resort, not first-line. Additionally: the most important 'treatment' for DPN is glycemic optimization to slow progression.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy + new floaters + vision blur — proliferative retinopathy referral urgency
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with longstanding T2DM (A1c consistently 9–10% for 5 years) had non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) at his last ophthalmology visit 10 months ago. He presents today reporting new-onset floaters in his right eye for 2 days and blurred vision that has worsened over 24 hours. He has no eye pain. VA is 20/80 in the right eye (was 20/30 at last visit). Which of the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Schedule routine ophthalmology follow-up within 3 months as planned
options.B: Prescribe topical prednisolone eye drops for diabetic macular edema pending ophthalmology visit
options.C: Refer urgently (same day) to ophthalmology — new floaters + progressive vision loss in known NPDR suggests progression to proliferative diabetic retinopathy with possible vitreous hemorrhage
options.D: Order CT head to evaluate for intracranial cause of visual disturbance

questions.rationale: New floaters + sudden vision loss in a patient with known NPDR = proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) with vitreous hemorrhage until proven otherwise. PDR involves abnormal neovascularization that is friable and bleeds into the vitreous — floaters and red/dark vision loss are classic. This is an ophthalmological emergency — same-day or next-day urgent retinal evaluation is required. Laser photocoagulation or anti-VEGF injection can prevent permanent vision loss if performed before severe hemorrhage or tractional retinal detachment. Routine 3-month follow-up (option A) would result in permanent vision loss. Topical steroids (option B) are not appropriate for vitreous hemorrhage. CT head (option D) is not the primary concern when there is a known diabetic eye disease in the setting of floaters — the retina must be evaluated urgently.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + low-cost medication access barrier + insulin fear + provider partnership — motivational interviewing approach
questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with T2DM (A1c 9.6%) on metformin refuses insulin despite the NP's recommendation. She states: 'My mother went on insulin and then went blind. I don't want to go that way.' She also mentions she is working two jobs and 'can't afford the monitoring supplies.' Which of the following best describes the most effective approach for this encounter?

options.A: Explain bluntly that insulin refusal at this A1c level will lead to blindness, kidney failure, and amputation
options.B: Accept her decision without further discussion and schedule follow-up in 3 months
options.C: Explore her fears about insulin using motivational interviewing; address cost barriers (manufacturer coupons, pharmacy programs, low-cost insulin options); discuss intermediate alternatives if insulin remains refused
options.D: Document that she is non-compliant and refer her to a diabetes educator for compliance counseling

questions.rationale: Motivational interviewing (MI) approach for medication refusal: explore ambivalence, validate concerns, and elicit the patient's own reasoning for change rather than confronting with fear-based messaging. Steps: (1) Reflective listening — acknowledge her fear about insulin from her mother's experience (correlating her mother's blindness with insulin rather than with uncontrolled diabetes, which is a common misattribution); (2) Address cost barriers specifically — $25 Walmart insulin (ReliOn NPH/Regular), manufacturer patient assistance programs, community pharmacy discount cards; (3) Explore alternatives — if insulin is still refused, could a SGLT-2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA reduce A1c significantly? This respects patient autonomy while maintaining the therapeutic partnership. Option A (fear-based messaging) typically increases resistance. Option B abandons therapeutic responsibility. Option D (labeling non-compliant) damages the therapeutic relationship.

questions.stemAnchor: T2DM + new microalbuminuria + ACEi started + SCr rose 22% in 4 weeks — acceptable vs unacceptable creatinine rise in DM
questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with T2DM, hypertension, and CKD (baseline creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, eGFR 48 mL/min) was started on lisinopril 5 mg daily for new microalbuminuria (UACR 82 mg/g). Four weeks later, creatinine is 1.59 mg/dL (eGFR 38 mL/min). K+ is 4.7 mEq/L. BP is 128/78 mmHg. She has no symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate response?

options.A: This creatinine rise (22%) is within the acceptable range; continue lisinopril and recheck BMP in 4–6 weeks
options.B: Discontinue lisinopril immediately — any creatinine rise after starting an ACEi in CKD indicates renal damage
options.C: Increase lisinopril to 10 mg to improve renoprotection and recheck creatinine in 2 weeks
options.D: Hold lisinopril and check a renal artery Doppler — a 22% rise suggests bilateral renal artery stenosis

questions.rationale: A creatinine rise of ≤30% from baseline after initiating ACEi/ARB in CKD is expected and acceptable — it reflects the intended hemodynamic effect (reduced glomerular hyperfiltration through efferent arteriole dilation), which is the mechanism of renoprotection. This patient: creatinine rose 22% (1.3→1.59) — below the 30% threshold. K+ 4.7 is safe (<5.0). Continue lisinopril and recheck BMP in 4–6 weeks to ensure stability. The 30% threshold for concern: if creatinine rises >30% → assess for dehydration, hold concurrent nephrotoxins, investigate for bilateral renal artery stenosis. Option B (stop for 22% rise) would deprive the patient of demonstrated renoprotection in diabetic nephropathy. Option D (renal artery Doppler) is warranted for >30% rise or bilateral RAS clinical suspicion — not for a 22% expected rise.
